explicit_text: Jim Hunt's campaign was very well organized, especially with Bert Bennett putting together his team. Unlike my approach, Hunt has been actively campaigning for the past six years and has built a strong political base through office holding. He also attended North Carolina State University, where he was involved in politics and the Young Democrats, which helped him develop a wide range of friends. His campaign strategy involved diligent county-by-county work, and he benefited from experienced political operatives who had helped me and Richardson Preyor in earlier campaigns.

explicit_text: Bert Bennett was the chief key operator for Jim Hunt's campaign, and he did an excellent job assembling a team. Bennett's work exemplified a more modern, politically based approach compared to my more civic enterprise base.

explicit_text: Bob Haines was chairman of the board of Wachovia and in earlier campaigns was responsible for raising substantial sums of money. However, I did not favor the old fundraising approach where a few big donors provided large amounts that were then distributed to counties. Instead, I preferred a quota system where county managers raised money locally to avoid obligations to special interests. Bob Haines represented the traditional fundraising model that we moved away from during my campaign.

explicit_text: Wachovia was connected to Bob Haines, who as chairman of the board played a role in traditional campaign fundraising. In the past, campaigns would rely on large sums raised by people like Haines and then allocate funds to counties. My campaign moved away from this model, preferring local fundraising quotas to avoid undue influence from big donors. Wachovia thus symbolizes the old way of financing campaigns that we sought to improve upon.

explicit_text: Luther Hodges was a figure from the era when the old fundraising approach was common, spanning from the 1930s through his time. Campaigns in his era often raised between fifty to a hundred thousand dollars from big donors, which was substantial then but insufficient for modern media campaigns. We changed that approach by implementing a quota system for counties to raise funds locally. Hodges' period represents the traditional political fundraising methods that we sought to reform.
